default_annotator.report_text:
JScript backdoor called Bateleur The Bateleur backdoor, written in JScript, is executed as part of the attack chain, allowing remote code execution.

The new macros and Bateleur backdoor use sophisticated anti-analysis and sandbox evasion techniques FIN7's Bateleur backdoor and the updated macros use sophisticated methods to detect and evade sandboxes or analysis environments.

the Vrst FIN7 change we observed was in the obfuscation technique found in their usual document attachments FIN7 changed their obfuscation techniques in the document attachments to hide malicious content.

drop a previously undocumented JScript backdoor, which we have named "Bateleur" FIN7 transitioned from their previous GGLDR script to a new JScript backdoor called Bateleur.execution.

The email is sent from an Outlook.com account, and the attachment document lure also matches that information by claiming "This document is encrypted by Outlook Protect Service". The email contains a malicious attachment designed to deceive the recipient into opening the file. The use of a service-specific lure, such as claiming encryption by Outlook Protect Service or Google Documents Protect Service, is a form of social engineering to persuade the victim to interact with the file.

The email contains a macro-laden Word document. The macro-laden Word document requires the user to open the file and enable the macro for execution, leading to the extraction of the JScript payload.

The caption contains a "|*|"-delimited obfuscated JScript payload The malicious payload is a JScript that is extracted and executed. JScript is a common scripting language used for automation and code execution.

The macro Vrst extracts the JScript from the caption then saves the content to debug.txt in the current user's temporary folder (%TMP%) The macro writes the extracted JScript payload to the debug.txt file in the temporary folder, likely with the intent to delete this file after execution to evade detection and forensic analysis.

the macro creates a scheduled task The macro creates a scheduled task to ensure the execution of the JScript payload.

execute debug.txt as a JScript. The malicious payload is a JScript that is extracted and executed. JScript is a common scripting language used for automation and code execution.

Finally, the macro sleeps for 10 seconds then deletes the malicious scheduled task his involves removing files that may indicate malicious activity, such as deleting scheduled tasks or other artifacts after they have been executed to avoid detection.

The malicious JScript has robust capabilities that include anti-sandbox functionality, anti-analysis (obfuscation) The adversary uses obfuscation to hide malicious code or make it harder to analyze.

retrieval of infected system information The malware gathers information about the infected system.

listing of running processes The malware lists running processes on the system.

execution of  custom commands and PowerShell scripts The malware uses PowerShell scripts for executing commands.

loading of EXEs and DLLs The malware loads executables (EXEs) and dynamic-link libraries (DLLs) into memory.

taking screenshots The malware takes screenshots of the infected system.

uninstalling and updating itself The malware can update itself by downloading new versions from the command and control server.

possibly the ability to exVltrate passwords, although the latter requires an additional module from the command and control server (C&C) The malware exfiltrates credentials (passwords) from the victim's machine, though it requires an additional module from the C&C server.

it creates a scheduled task "GoogleUpdateTaskMachineSystem" for persistence using the following command pattern: schtasks /Create /f /tn "GoogleUpdateTaskMachineSystem" /tr "wscript.exe //b /e:jscript C:\Users\[user account]\AppData\Local\Temp\{[hex]-[hex]-[hex]-[hex]-[hex]}\debug.txt" /sc minute /mo 5 This technique involves the use of scheduled tasks to execute programs or scripts at a specific time or interval for persistence.

it creates a scheduled task "GoogleUpdateTaskMachineSystem" for persistence using the following command pattern: schtasks /Create /f /tn "GoogleUpdateTaskMachineSystem" /tr "wscript.exe //b /e:jscript C:\Users\[user account]\AppData\Local\Temp\{[hex]-[hex]-[hex]-[hex]-[hex]}\debug.txt" /sc minute /mo 5 a legitimate name is used for the scheduled task

Bateleur has anti-sandbox features but they do not appear to be used at this time.  This includes detection of Virtualbox, VMware, or Parallels via SMBIOSBIOSVersion and any of the following strings in DeviceID This technique involves malware checking the environment for signs of being run in a virtualized or sandboxed environment to avoid detection or analysis. Common checks include identifying virtual machines or sandbox-specific indicators.

Return running process list (name + id) Listing the processes that are currently running on a system.

Return various information about the infected machine, such as computer and domain name, OS, screen size, This technique involves gathering information about the system to better understand the environment or to gather information useful for further attacks. This can include details like the computer name, operating system, and more.

net view This technique involves scanning network services to gather information about the network and potentially identify other systems and

Delete installation Vle and path and remove scheduled task Removing installation file and path and remove scheduled tasks.

Perform a "load_exe" request to the C&C to retrieve an EXE This technique involves transferring tools or other files to a compromised system. In this case, the load_exe request to the command and control (C&C) server to retrieve an executable file.

write a cmd.exe command to a Vle named debug.cmd and then execute debug.cmd with cmd.exe This technique involves writing and executing commands or scripts. In this case, writing a cmd.exe command to a file and then executing it.

Perform a "load_exe" request to C&C to retrieve an EXE, save it as debug.log the load_exe request to the command and control (C&C) server to retrieve an executable file.

execute the EXE via WMI This technique involves using Windows Management Instrumentation (WMI) to execute commands or scripts. Here, WMI is used to execute the retrieved EXE.

Perform a "load_dll" request to the C&C to retrieve a DLL, save it as debug.backup the load_dll request to the command and control (C&C) server retrieves a DLL.

write a regsvr32 command to a Vle named debug.cmd and then execute debug.cmd with cmd.exe a regsvr32 command is written to a file and then executed.

Perform a "load_cmd" request to the C&C to retrieve a command to execute A command file is retrieved from the command and control (C&C) server.

create temp Vle named log_[date].cmd containing command to execute, execute the command and sleep for 55 seconds a command is executed via a batch file

Send Vle output to the C&C via a POST request Using web protocols (HTTP/HTTPS) for command and control communication. Sending the output of the executed command to the C&C server.

remove the temporary command Vle removing the temporary command file

Perform a "load_powershell" request to the C&C to retrieve a command to execute A command file is retrieved from the command and control (C&C) server.

create a temp Vle named log_[date].log containing a PowerShell command to execute, execute the command, and sleep for 55 seconds Executing commands via PowerShell.

Send Vle output to the C&C via a POST request Sending the output of the executed command to the C&C server.

executes a PowerShell command via WMI Using WMI to execute commands

Take a screenshot and save it as screenshot.png in the install_path Capturing screenshots of the victim's machine.

Perform a "load_pass" request to the C&C to retrieve a PowerShell command A command file is retrieved from the command and control (C&C) server.

PowerShell command containing a payload A PowerShell command containing a payload

capable of retrieving user account credentials Retrieving credentials

The Bateleur C&C protocol occurs over HTTPS and is fairly straightforward with no additional encoding or obfuscation. Bateleur uses HTTP POST requests with a URI of "/?page=wait" while the backdoor is waiting for instructions. Bateleur communicates with its C&C server using web protocols (HTTPS and HTTP POST), which is common for exfiltrating data and receiving commands.
